text: A hiatal hernia is when the top of the stomach gets displaced or herniates through the diaphragm and up into the chest cavity. Although most people with a hiatal hernia do not experience any symptoms, others may suffer from recurrent symptomatic reflux requiring medication or even surgery.
The surgeon pulls the stomach back into its normal position below the diaphragm, reduces the size of the diaphragmatic opening, and places a mesh over the incision to reinforce the closure.
The surgical footage shows the dissection of the stomach, esophagus, and hernia sac from the surrounding chest and abdominal soft tissue structures during a hiatal hernia repair.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the hernia sac and esophagus, ensuring identification and preservation of the vagal nerves. The esophagus is dissected to achieve at least three centimeters of distal esophagus positioned within the abdomen, utilizing a high mediastinal dissection technique.

The surgeon closes the enlarged diaphragmatic opening with sutures, ensuring a tension-free repair. A mesh is placed over the incision to reinforce the closure, reducing the risk of hiatal hernia recurrence.

A hiatal hernia repair is often performed in combination with some variation of fundoplication whether nissen or toupee to further control severe reflux. In some cases, hiatal hernia surgery can also be combined with weight loss surgery such as a sleeve gastrectomy.
